data.title: Nova Minerals: Solid Capital Injection And Possible Mineral Production In 2026
data.description:
The race for critical minerals continues, fueled by factors such as heightened federal activity, including direct equity investments in companies like Lithium Americas and MP Materials, as well as policy implementation to support domestic production. Australian company Nova Minerals Limited (NVA), known for its exploration of gold and antimony, gained recognition in October 2025 after receiving a $43.4 million non-dilutive award from the US Department of War for antimony production. The stock is up 195.23% (YoY) and has a market cap of $260 million. In this article, I will explain why Nova Minerals is a hold in view of its liquidity portfolio that will enable it attain its development targets by FY 2026, but is overvalued.

NVA is focused on a solid capital injection in 2026 that I believe will be crucial in ensuring it achieves its production targets, especially in the Estelle Project. Firstly, as of September 2025, NVA explained that it had a total of AUD 86.3 million ($57.75 million) in funding. It included its cash balance of AUD 17.5 million ($11.71 million), the DoW grant of $43.4 million, and a liquid investment of $3.3 million. Additionally, in late December 2025, NVA announced the closure of a public offering of about 2.93 million “American Depositary Shares (ADS) priced at $6.83 per ADS” grossing $20 million. This means that NVA has a total liquidity of about $77.75 million as of 2026.

That said, NVA confirmed in its recent financial report that the Antimony project was fully funded by the DoW’s $43.4 million grant. We must also keep in mind that it is a 24-month award that is supposed to run until 2027, by which time it will have already begun production. It leaves a balance of $34.35 million, which is intended to be used for tier-one gold asset development in FY 2026/27. In FY 2025, the capitalized exploration expenditure (CAPEX) used in the Estelle gold project increased 9% (YoY) to A$100.135 million from A$92.118 million. A 20% (YoY) increase in the CapEx would mean Nova will be required to spend about A$120.16 million ($80.423 million) as CapEx in 2026. The CapEx required would be more than this into 2026 if the company decides to acquire more antimony assets in Pakistan, as has been alleged in various media articles. Keeping in mind that NVA's US domestic antimony production is fully funded for production by 2027. However, the company recently clarified that its “focus was to complete the feasibility study for its gold assets and begin the antimony trisulfide production.” Taking this allegation into consideration, the 100+ tons supposedly to be purchased from Pakistan in Q1 2026 would mean that a ton of antimony would cost about $20,000 (seeing that the first purchase price is expected to be $2 million). Critical to the company is that production of antimony must commence in 2026 as per the DoW funding agreement.

The global antimony market was valued at $2.35 billion in 2025 and is expected to reach $4.85 billion by 2035 (growing at a CAGR of 7.5% within the forecast period). The research also shows that 50% of the antimony produced globally is in Asia, with China controlling the bulk of the production. The demand for antimony has been aggravated by its use in the manufacturing of solar panels, semiconductors, batteries, and ammunition. As it appears, the DoW’s injection appears to be part of the US plan to insulate itself from outside critical mineral sources and boost its ammunition.

It is also vital to note that NVA will seek to first produce the “military-grade antimony trisulfide (Sb₂S₃) for which it received the funding until 2027, before it expands to antimony oxides and the antimony metal itself, which are priced differently. Into 2026, the price of antimony trisulfide (Sb2S3) stands at $51.86 per Kg (in North America) and $22.05 per Kg in Northeast Asia. The 7N High Purity Antimony goes for an average of $385.42 per kg, while the 99.5% Antimony trioxide goes for an average of $17,249 per MT.

For the FY 2026, Nova will need more money as it will also involve seeking permits alongside conducting the feasibility studies of the gold assets. Construction of the Estelle gold mining infrastructure, including resource expansion, is expected to commence in 2028. The US Antimony Corp announced a total contract award of $352 million in late 2025 (from the US Defense Logistics Agency and an Industrial customer) to also produce antimony, and into 2026, they indicated that they are sourcing feedstock from as far as Mexico, as opposed to Alaska.

While the Estelle mining project owned by Nova Minerals does not yet have proven gold reserves (as elucidated in this previous article), it sits in the same geological zone as the Fort Knox mine, owned by Kinross Gold (within the Alaskan area of the Tintina Gold belt). As of the nine months ending on September 30, 2025, Kinross Gold reported that the Fort Knox mine produced 339,299 ounces of gold, up 24.58% (YoY) from 272,357 ounces recorded in the prior year. Sales of $432.8 million realized from Fort Knox (at the time) represented 60.7% of all the revenue Kinross Gold obtained in the US alone, and was the highest in the company’s overall numbers. Since 1996, the company has produced over 7 million ounces of gold (29 years), up 183% from a proven and probable reserves of 230 million tons at 0.3 g/t of gold (or 2.471 million ounces) researched back in 2018. NVA’s resource base stands at a probable 9.9 million ounces in its lifetime, and as seen above, it could increase by 50%-100% or even more (since the feasibility study is ongoing). Keeping in mind that gold production is likely to start in 2028/2029. If the reserves are proven by then, we are likely to see the first annual production range of 300,000 ounces to 500,000 ounces (a midpoint of 400,000 ounces). With the current gold price at about $4,463 per ounce, a production cost of about $2,000 per ounce would mean NVA rakes in an annual sales return of $800 million in the FY 2028/2029. However, this will only be possible if the gold reserves are proven, as at present the company is still in the development stages.

Valuation

I will use the price-to-book ratio because NVA is at the pre-revenue stage (yet to hit commercialization) as it is an exploration and development company. This ratio will evaluate the company’s market price (per share) against its net assets or book value as of 2026.

NVA’s price-to-book (forward) multiple stands at 3.89X. To calculate the market price per share, I will need to identify the forward net assets for FY 2025. Firstly, the net assets for FY 2025 were $109.86 million, indicating an 11.66% (YoY) increase in the net asset value.

As stated earlier, Nova Minerals is expected to start commercialization in 2026, through the production of its first batch of antimony (including possible positive results from gold feasibility studies). NVA's peer, Perpetua Resources, had its net assets increase by almost 600% (YoY) as of September 2025. Perpetua Resources is set to begin construction of its Stibnite Gold -Antimony Project in Idaho in 2026, a move that will increase its asset cover in the year. NVA is also set to commence its production of antimony in 2026 that will also increase its net asset value in the year.

A closer look at NVA shows that since FY 2021, NVA’s net assets have increased by about 108.93% to 2025. By inference, an increase of approximately 100% in its net assets in FY 2026 (with the commencement of production in mind) will likely result in a future net asset value of A$219.71 million ($146.85 million) in FY 2026. To determine the future market share price, I will multiply the forward net asset value ($440.23 million) by the price-to-book ratio (3.89 times), resulting in a future market capitalization of $571.25 million. NVA’s outstanding share count sits at 401.5 million shares. To get the future share price, I will divide the market cap by the outstanding share count, resulting in $1.42 as the future share price. NVA’s stock price stood at $7.36 as of this writing, giving a difference of -418% (YoY). While the analysis shows overvaluation by more than 400%, it is working on a P/B ratio of 3.89X and a 100% (YoY) growth in net assets. Growth beyond this point will increase the stock's value. That is, if net asset value grows by 1000%, then the forward total equity will be $810 million, thereby driving the price towards $7.85.

I will compare the performance of NVA with one of its peers, Perpetua Resources Corp. (PPTA), which is in the gold and antimony business but has yet to make revenues. It is vital to first mention that PPTA operates in the Idaho Area of the Stibnite Gold Project (with revenues expected from 2027/2028). As of September 2025, its NAV stood at $532 million, an increase of 589.2% (YoY) from $77.2 million in the prior year. An increase of about 50% (YoY) would mean PPTA’s total equity value in 2026 will sit at $798 million. We have to keep in mind that NVA is likely to introduce new antimony assets in 2026, while PPTA will probably wait until FY 2027/28.

Forward P/B ratio

Forward NAV (Equity)

Future Market Cap

Future share price

Current price

Valuation
NVA 3.89X $440.23 million $1.712 billion $1.42 $7.36 -418 (YoY) overvalued
PPTA 5.68X $798 million $4.533 billion $37.18 $27.50 +35.21% (YoY) undervalued

The above tabulated analysis shows that PPTA is undervalued by 35.21% (YoY) while NVA is overvalued by 418%. I have confined the analysis to the price-to-book ratio since the company is pre-revenue. The growth in the total equity has also been calculated at a minimum growth rate of 100% (YoY). It is important to consider the possible production of antimony in 2026 and other constructions that will add revenues and assets to the company.

Commencement of military-grade antimony production by NVA in 2026 may increase the company’s chances of commercialization by late 2026/2027 and thereby raise the NAV in 2026.

Risks

This valuation works on the premise that Nova Minerals will commence the production of antimony in 2026, as previously indicated. This production has informed by the decision to increase the NAV by more than 1000% due to the introduction of new assets with higher valuation by the company in 2026. Failure to commence this production will adversely affect the share price as elucidated herein. Further, I also expect positive outcomes from the gold feasibility studies in 2026, which are expected to guide the financing and construction into 2028.

Lastly, the company may dilute its stock to raise capital, especially for conducting the gold feasibility studies, if no other funding sources are secured. In the year ending June 2025, NVA increased its outstanding share balance by 64.82% (YoY) to 401.5 million shares from 243.6 million shares.

So NVA has about 401.5 million in the FY 2025, having closed the FY 2024 with 243.6 million shares. It represents a dilution of about 64%. Still, the company's share momentum in 2025 has been robust, as the stock has an upside of almost 200% (as of this writing). We need to consider the fundamentals that have been discussed in this article, keeping in mind that the company is moving towards a production (of antimony) that is fully funded.

While share dilution remains a major risk for NVA (especially if it runs out of cash), the company has zero debt. Consequently, if it lacks any other funding sources for its gold feasibility project, then it will either go for debt or dilute its stock. I expect more information on this later in March 2026, when the company will likely confirm its commencement of antimony production and progress of the gold feasibility study.

Bottom Line

I have rated Nova Minerals as a hold in light of its robust liquidity portfolio that will likely see it attain its antimony production and gold feasibility studies in 2026. However, the stock is overvalued based on my price-to-book analysis, and I have assumed a possible 100% growth in its total equity or net asset value by 2026. The main risks to this analysis are the failure to commence the antimony production on time, which may hurt the company’s commercialization process and possibly result in share dilution to raise funding in 2026. I await the next earnings date in March 2026 to update my analysis.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JQUA: Balanced Approach With Lighter Allocation To Mega Caps (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The JPMorgan U.S. Quality Factor ETF (JQUA) is a fund that goes beyond a quality-oriented strategy; it also seeks a balanced sector mix and a portfolio construction that gives the fund a relatively conservative profile compared to the equity indexes, such as the Russell 1000. While the sector exposure has not changed much since my last review of the fund in September 2024, its allocation to mega caps has decreased somewhat, exacerbating a mid- and large-cap tilt that may differentiate the fund’s performance from here. The coming sections will break down JQUA’s portfolio across core sectors and compare measures such as valuation, performance, volatility, and tradability with the broader market and other quality ETFs

ETF Description & Highlights

JQUA is an ETF that seeks to enhance returns by investing in U.S. companies with above-average quality and profitability, tracking the JP Morgan US Quality Factor Index.

This index follows a rules-based methodology that selects stocks from the Russell 1000 based on a proprietary quality factor determined through measures including profitability, earnings, and solvency. Stocks are selected across all sectors and weighted according to their market capitalization, while targeting a neutral sector exposure relative to the Russell 1000. The index is also rebalanced on a quarterly basis to reflect current market conditions.

As of December 31, 2025, JQUA had $7.6 billion in assets invested across 209 holdings, with an average market cap of $114.8 billion, which is nearly one-third of the Russell 1000 average market cap of $354.8 billion. With 39.2% of the fund in large caps and 29.4% in mid caps, these two market cap categories make up the majority of the portfolio, while mega caps (18.0%) and small caps (12.3%) correspond to a smaller share of the fund. This highlights a shift away from mega caps compared to my last update on this fund in September 2024, when mega caps accounted for 27.2% of total assets. JQUA’s current market cap profile also marks a contrast with the Russell 1000, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), where mega caps make up 42.2% of the index, due to its market cap-weighted structure.

JQUA’s top ten holdings are NVIDIA (2.05%), Microsoft (2.00%), Berkshire Hathaway (1.98%), Meta Platforms (1.96%), Apple (1.95%), Alphabet (1.95%), Visa (1.91%), Broadcom (1.77%), Johnson & Johnson (1.63%), and Exxon Mobil (1.56%). Together, these positions make up just under 20% of total assets and are concentrated in mega caps from technology and communications services, similar to the broader equity indexes, though these top holdings represent a much higher share of the Russell 1000, for example, where NVIDIA sits near 7%, with Apple and Microsoft each above 5%. The list is complemented by names from financial services, healthcare, and energy, like Visa, Johnson & Johnson, and Exxon Mobil, which give the fund a slightly more balanced distribution than the index.

The following table introduces a list of quality-oriented funds that will serve as the peer group for JQUA in this analysis. The first is the iShares MSCI USA Quality Factor ETF (QUAL), the largest fund in the group by assets under management, tracking the MSCI USA Sector Neutral Quality Index, which uses profitability, earnings stability, and leverage metrics while maintaining sector neutrality. The Invesco S&P 500 Quality ETF (SPHQ) applies quality screens such as ROE, accruals, and financial leverage to the S&P 500 constituents, resulting in a predominantly large-cap profile with market-cap-based weighting. The Dimensional US High Profitability ETF (DUHP) takes a more flexible approach, emphasizing high operating profitability across large and mid caps without formally tracking an index, while the Fidelity Quality Factor ETF (FQAL) tracks a proprietary quality index focused on profitability and earnings consistency. Completing the list, the Vanguard U.S. Quality Factor ETF (VFQY) blends quality metrics with a more pronounced mid- and small-cap footprint, and the Astoria US Equal Weight Quality Kings ETF (ROE) filters 100 stocks based on quality measures and adopts an equal-weight approach.

From a sector allocation perspective, JQUA has 37.2% invested in technology, followed by financial services (13.9%) and consumer discretionary (11.4%), healthcare (9.6%), and industrials (7.5%). Smaller positions go to communication services (6.9%), consumer staples (5.5%), energy (3.2%), and real estate (2.1%), with basic materials (1.5%) and utilities (1.3%) rounding out the portfolio. As a result, relative to the Russell 1000, JQUA is most overweight in technology (+3.6%), offset by underweight exposure to communication services (-3.7%). Despite these gaps, it is fair to say that JQUA has a sector mix that largely mirrors the Russell 1000, which is consistent with its targeted neutral sector positioning relative to the benchmark. Of note, the fund’s sector weights have also largely stayed unchanged since my last article in September 2024, with minor changes such as an increase in technology (+4.1%) primarily due to the sector’s outperformance during this period.

If we break down JQUA’s portfolio across its core sectors, we see that holdings in technology highlight an overweight exposure to the software segment, through positions in Microsoft, Salesforce, Palantir, the data analytics provider, and ServiceNow, the workflow automation platform. Semiconductors are also a meaningful pillar, though they represent a lighter share compared to the Russell 1000. Key holdings are Nvidia, Broadcom, AMD, and Lam Research. Other positions in the sector include Apple, data storage supplier Western Digital, enterprise flash storage provider Pure Storage, and hybrid cloud storage player NetApp.

Holdings in financial services look more balanced with 37 positions across payments, insurance, and market infrastructure, while steering clear of the banking segment. Diversified conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway is the largest sector position, while holdings in consumer finance are also significant through global payment giants Visa and Mastercard. The fund owns insurers such as Chubb, the commercial insurer, Progressive, the auto insurer, Travelers, the P&C carrier, and Arch Capital, the specialty insurer. Holdings in market data and exchanges round out the allocation to financials via names like S&P Global, the ratings and index provider, Intercontinental Exchange, the exchange operator, and Cboe Global Markets, the options exchange player.

JQUA owns 35 companies in consumer discretionary, with a diversified allocation in segments such as travel, dining, and retail. The travel-related exposure is led by global hotel operator Marriott, hotel chain Hilton, online travel agency Expedia, and Booking Holdings. The fund owns restaurant franchisor Yum Brands, McDonald’s, the quick-service leader, and Starbucks, the coffeehouse operator. Holdings in home improvement are concentrated in two household names: Home Depot and Lowe’s, while the specialty retail sleeve includes auto parts retailer O’Reilly Automotive, Ulta Beauty, Best Buy, and pool supplies distributor Pool Corp.

Outside the top three sectors, significant allocations include Alphabet and Meta Platforms in communication services, energy giants Exxon Mobil, Chevron, and ConocoPhillips, consumer staples names such as Procter & Gamble, Colgate-Palmolive, and Philip Morris International, along with retailers Costco and Walmart. Elsewhere, JQUA still owns industrials like Illinois Tool Works, Lincoln Electric, and Waste Management, as well as Linde, the industrial gases supplier in the basic materials sector.

JQUA’s sector mix is arguably highly correlated with most peers. This includes QUAL (0.99), FQAL (0.99), ROE (0.98), and DUHP (0.94), as these funds share a generally neutral allocation compared to broader equity indexes, with over 30% exposure to technology, a low teens position in financial services and consumer discretionary, along with mid to high-single stances in industrials and consumer staples.

Meanwhile, SPHQ and VFQY keep a more conservative allocation profile, with technology below 30%, while maintaining overweight exposure to defensive areas such as consumer staples and healthcare, and also in industrials. Still, sector correlation remains in the 0.70-0.80 range with JQUA, as they keep a fairly large position in core sectors like financial services and healthcare, for instance.

Mid-Range Valuation

JQUA’s 21.0x P/E represents a small 3% discount to the Russell 1000’s 21.7x. The fund’s valuation remains roughly in line with the benchmark, as sector allocation gaps are not so meaningful. In addition, the fund’s stock selection keeps individual sectors’ P/E ratios relatively close to the benchmark as well. The only representative gaps stem from consumer discretionary and healthcare, where holdings trade at 66% and 18% discounts, respectively. This is driven by a few factors, such as no positions in Tesla, Amazon, and Eli Lilly, all trading above 30x P/E, as well as overweight allocations in value plays like Merck (11.9x P/E), Bristol Myers (8.2x), ExxonMobil (17.6x), and Qualcomm (14.3x).

The fund’s growth picture is also mixed. Its historical earnings growth of 9.2% is above the Russell 1000’s 8.2%, driven by earnings growth in financial services and healthcare that has been much higher than the index, driven by overweight positions in names like Idexx and Merck in healthcare and a number of holdings in financial services, including Jack Henry and Fidelity National Financial. The flip side is JQUA’s sales growth of 6.9%, which trails the benchmark’s 7.4%, reflecting widespread slower growth across most sectors, particularly technology, healthcare, and consumer discretionary, and reflecting a portfolio with lighter exposure to high-growth companies such as NVIDIA, Amazon, and Microsoft.

JQUA is close to the Russell 1000 on profitability, but still falls short of the benchmark. Its EBITDA margin of 31.9% lags the Russell 1000’s 36.0% by about 4.1 percentage points, while ROE of 41.2% trails the benchmark’s 43.3%. The technology sector is the weak spot, dragging down the fund’s profitability average, despite relatively stronger numbers in other sectors. This was mostly due to underweight positions in high-margin big techs like Apple, NVIDIA, and Microsoft.

JQUA’s valuation analysis relative to peers can be split into two steps. First, the fund trades at a discount to QUAL (23.6x P/E), SPUQ (22.2x), DUHP (21.4x), and FQAL (22.9x), largely due to these funds’ heavier allocation to large and mega caps at nearly 70%, while JQUA keeps it below 60%, with positions in mid caps (29.4%) and small caps (12.3%) driving valuation down.

On the flip side, VFQY and ROE trade at much lower P/E multiples. This reflects their stronger tilt toward small and mid caps, totaling 69% and 49% of assets, respectively, leading to a valuation profile that resembles a mid-cap fund in the 15x to 17x range. In sum, JQUA’s valuation sits in the middle of the pack, primarily influenced by market cap distribution, as sector mix does not play a key role here, given its somewhat neutral sector exposure.

Performance In Line With Peers' Average

JQUA has performed in line with the Russell in the long term, with a 14.0% annualized return over the last five years. But the fund has lagged more recently. Its 3-year annualized return of 19.1% has not kept pace with the benchmark’s 22.7%, after a strong underperformance in 2024 and 2025, likely driven by its underweight exposure to big techs that led the broader market over this period. Compared to peers, JQUA has delivered returns broadly in line with the group average. However, we can see a clear bifurcation in performance, especially over the 3-year time frame, with QUAL, SPHQ, and FQAL clearly outperforming. Of note, it is fair to say that DUHP and ROE have a limited track record, and more time is needed to better evaluate their performance over a full market cycle.

The fund has seen moderate volatility, with betas in the 0.91-0.92 range, below the Russell 1000 and most peers, though slightly above SPHQ, which is a fund with a more defensive profile, as illustrated by its underweight allocation to technology, for instance. JQUA has also shown a relatively contained price action relative to the broader market, with capture ratios below 1.00. This is particularly evidenced during price declines, as its downside capture ratio in the 95-98 range has been the second lowest, only above SPHQ, which is consistent with the lower betas of these two funds compared to the rest of the peer group.

JQUA's average daily trading volume of $34.4 million is decent, though clearly below funds with heavy volume like QUAL and SPHQ. Still, the fund maintains a tight 0.02% bid-ask spread, which is narrower than most peers. Its 21% turnover is the second lowest in the group, leading to a low 0.12% expense ratio, the lowest among peers. The fund offers a 1.19% dividend yield, slightly higher than peers, though still modest in absolute terms. Its tracking error is slightly higher than the peer group average, while Sharpe ratios of 1.18 (3-year) and 0.73 (5-year) sit near the middle of the peer group, in line with QUAL, but below SPHQ and FQAL over the 3-year window, largely influenced by their strong performance that has played a major role in risk-adjusted returns.

Final Thoughts

Based on an allocation strategy that targets a neutral sector exposure, JQUA maintains a fairly large exposure to sectors such as technology and consumer discretionary, but individual positions in big tech names are much lighter than in broader market indexes, like the Russell 1000. Still, valuations are close to this benchmark, though below some of the mega-cap heavy ETF peers. Meanwhile, with a backdrop that still looks supportive in the sense that inflation appears to be easing and a market that is sitting near new highs, despite potential headwinds like slower growth and trade headlines, my view is that investors are likely to be more selective throughout 2026, which creates a good setup for quality stocks.

With that in mind, JQUA looks well-positioned in this environment, especially if leadership broadens beyond big techs. On the other hand, the fund’s recent underperformance is a reminder of the downside associated with an investment strategy that seems to avoid concentration in any particular stocks, as its top holding, NVIDIA, represents only 2% of total assets. That said, with relatively fair valuation and solid earnings growth, JQUA is a good fit and a long-term hold for risk-aware investors, as illustrated by its below-average volatility, but is probably not the best alternative for those seeking high-momentum strategies. Still, assuming the S&P 500’s earnings growth of 12% for 2026, it is reasonable to expect the fund to deliver a high-single digit to low-teens total return this year, just a bit above the fund’s historical average, considering limited room for P/E multiple expansion, given historically elevated valuations in the overall stock market.
